title: The Second Sunday of Advent
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
Sermon Date: December 9, 2018
Preacher: Richard Lawson

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon was preached on the Second Sunday of Advent in Year B of the three-year Revised Common Lectionary cycle. The readings likely included Isaiah 40:1-11 (comfort and preparation), 2 Peter 3:8-15a (patience and holiness), and Mark 1:1-8 (John the Baptist's ministry of repentance). The Advent season emphasizes preparation for Christ's coming, with readings focusing on repentance, patience, and the call to prepare spiritually—themes particularly resonant with John the Baptist's wilderness ministry.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the aftermath of its 79th General Convention held in Austin, Texas in July 2018, which had addressed controversial issues including marriage equality and transgender inclusion. The Diocese of Colorado was led by Bishop Robert J. O'Neill at this time. No major diocesan crises or bishop elections were occurring in early December 2018, though national conversations about Anglican identity and progressive social positions continued to shape denominational discourse.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing early winter weather in early December 2018, with temperatures dropping into the 20s-30s Fahrenheit range typical for that time of year. The city was focused on preparations for the holiday season, with the Denver Christkindl Market opening in mid-November drawing crowds downtown. The broader political climate in Colorado remained divided following the 2018 midterm elections in November, where Democrats gained significant ground in statewide races, reflecting the state's shifting demographics.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The week before December 9 was dominated by the ongoing Mueller investigation into Russian interference in the 2016 election, with Michael Cohen's sentencing scheduled for mid-December drawing intense media attention. The Trump administration faced criticism over climate policy and environmental rollbacks, particularly relevant given Colorado's energy sector concerns. The national political atmosphere was contentious as the year-end holiday season approached, with partisan divides deepening over immigration policy and the border.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

France was experiencing major civil unrest from the Yellow Vest (Gilets Jaunes) protests that erupted in late November over fuel taxes and cost-of-living issues, with violent clashes between demonstrators and police continuing into early December. The UN climate conference (COP24) was being held in Katowice, Poland during this exact week (December 3-14, 2018), with nations negotiating climate change commitments. Brexit negotiations continued to create uncertainty in the United Kingdom as Prime Minister Theresa May navigated Parliament's objections to her withdrawal agreement.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

American culture in early December 2018 was characterized by holiday commercialism competing with deep political polarization and anxiety about institutional stability. The #MeToo movement remained culturally prominent as allegations against powerful figures continued to emerge and affect entertainment, politics, and media. Denver residents would have been aware of the Broncos' disappointing 2018 NFL season (the team finished 6-10), reflecting a broader sense of uncertainty in the city; simultaneously, holiday traditions and shopping dominated public attention, creating a dissonance between seasonal cheer and underlying social tension.
